name: Box Breathing
description: A calming 4-4-4-4 breathing pattern used by Navy SEALs and first responders to reduce stress quickly.

steps.instruction: Sit comfortably with your back straight. Place your hands on your knees. Close your eyes or soften your gaze.
Exhale completely through your mouth, emptying your lungs.
Inhale slowly through your nose for 4 counts: 1… 2… 3… 4.
Hold your breath for 4 counts: 1… 2… 3… 4.
Exhale slowly through your mouth for 4 counts: 1… 2… 3… 4.
Hold empty for 4 counts: 1… 2… 3… 4. Repeat the cycle 3 more times.

name: 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding
description: A sensory grounding technique that anchors you to the present moment by engaging all five senses.

steps.instruction: Take a slow breath and look around. Name 5 things you can SEE right now — a wall, your hands, a window, anything.
Name 4 things you can TOUCH. Reach out and feel them — your chair, your clothing, a surface nearby.
Pause and name 3 things you can HEAR — traffic, your own breathing, background noise.
Name 2 things you can SMELL. If nothing is obvious, notice the neutral scent of air or your skin.
Name 1 thing you can TASTE. Take a breath and notice any taste in your mouth. Take another slow breath and return to the moment.

name: Deep Belly Breathing
description: Simple diaphragmatic breathing to activate your parasympathetic nervous system.

steps.instruction: Place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. As you breathe, your belly should rise, not your chest.
Inhale slowly through your nose for 4 seconds, letting your belly expand.
Exhale slowly through pursed lips for 6 seconds, letting your belly fall. Repeat 6 times.

name: 4-7-8 Breathing
description: Dr. Andrew Weil's relaxation breath that calms the nervous system in minutes.

steps.instruction: Sit with your back straight. Place the tip of your tongue behind your upper front teeth and keep it there.
Exhale completely through your mouth with a whoosh sound.
Inhale quietly through your nose for 4 counts.
Hold your breath for 7 counts.
Exhale completely through your mouth with a whoosh for 8 counts. This is one cycle. Repeat 3 more times.

name: Body Scan
description: A mindfulness practice that releases tension by bringing awareness to each part of your body.

steps.instruction: Lie down or sit comfortably. Close your eyes. Take 3 slow breaths.
Bring your attention to the top of your head. Notice any sensations — warmth, tingling, or tension. Simply observe.
Move your awareness down to your face, jaw, and neck. Soften any tightness you notice there.
Shift to your shoulders, arms, and hands. Let them get heavy and relaxed.
Move to your chest, belly, and back. With each exhale, let them release.
Finally, bring attention to your hips, legs, and feet. Breathe gently. Notice your whole body feeling heavier and calmer.

name: Progressive Muscle Relaxation
description: Tense and release muscle groups to release stored physical tension.

steps.instruction: Sit or lie comfortably. Take 2 slow breaths to begin.
Clench your hands into fists, hold for 5 seconds, then release completely. Feel the contrast.
Scrunch your face tight — forehead, eyes, jaw — hold 5 seconds, then release.
Shrug your shoulders up to your ears, hold 5 seconds, then drop them completely.
Tighten your core, hold 5 seconds, release. Arch your back gently, hold 5 seconds, release.
Tighten your legs and point your toes, hold 5 seconds, then release everything. Rest and breathe slowly.

name: Mindful Observation
description: Focus on a single object to quiet your mind and return to the present.

steps.instruction: Pick any object in front of you — a cup, your phone, a plant.
Look at it as if seeing it for the first time. Notice its shape, color, texture, and edges.
If your mind wanders, gently return to the object. Keep observing for 2 full minutes without judgment.

name: Three Good Things
description: Shift your mindset by consciously noticing the positive in your day.

steps.instruction: Close your eyes or look somewhere soft. Take a breath.
Think of ONE thing that went okay today — big or small. A warm drink, a kind word, sunlight.
Think of a SECOND good thing. Let yourself feel a small warmth for it.
Think of a THIRD good thing. Breathe into gratitude for a moment before returning.

name: Self-Compassion Break
description: Dr. Kristin Neff's practice to treat yourself with the kindness you'd offer a friend.

steps.instruction: Place a hand on your heart or somewhere soothing. Acknowledge: 'This is a moment of suffering.'
Remind yourself: 'Suffering is part of being human. I am not alone in this.'
Ask yourself: what would I say to a close friend going through this exact thing?
Say those words to yourself now. Quietly or aloud. Let them in.
Rest with your hand on your heart. Breathe slowly. You deserve kindness too.

name: Cold Water Reset
description: Splashing cold water activates the dive reflex, slowing your heart rate rapidly.

steps.instruction: Go to a sink. Hold cold water in your hands or fill a bowl.
Lean forward, close your eyes, and splash cold water on your face 3–5 times.
Pat dry gently. Breathe slowly. Notice how your body feels more alert and calm.

name: Shake It Out
description: Animals instinctively shake off stress after threat — we can too.

steps.instruction: Stand up if you can. Shake your hands loosely for 15 seconds.
Let the shake travel up your arms, into your shoulders, down your body.
Bounce gently on your heels for 20 seconds, letting your whole body jiggle.
Stop and stand still. Notice the tingling aliveness in your body. Take 3 slow breaths.

name: Counting Breaths
description: A simple anchor practice: count breaths from 1 to 10, then start again.

steps.instruction: Sit comfortably. Let your breath be natural — don't force it.
Breathe in. On the exhale, count '1'. Breathe in. Exhale: '2'. Continue to '10'.
If your mind wanders and you lose count, gently return to '1' without frustration.
Complete 3–4 full rounds of counting 1 to 10. Let each breath be a small rest.

name: Calm Place Visualization
description: Create a vivid mental sanctuary to retreat to whenever stress peaks.

steps.instruction: Close your eyes. Take 3 deep breaths.
Imagine a place where you feel completely safe and at peace — a beach, a forest, a favorite room.
See it clearly: the colors, the light, the shapes around you.
Hear the sounds there — waves, birds, wind, or silence.
Feel the air on your skin — its temperature, its movement.
Stay for a minute. When you're ready, take a breath and slowly open your eyes.

name: Neck & Shoulder Release
description: Stress collects in your neck and shoulders. These gentle stretches release it.

steps.instruction: Sit upright. Slowly drop your right ear toward your right shoulder. Hold 20 seconds, breathing.
Slowly roll your head to center, then drop your left ear to your left shoulder. Hold 20 seconds.
Drop your chin to your chest gently. Feel the stretch in the back of your neck. Hold 20 seconds.
Roll your shoulders backward in slow circles 5 times, then forward 5 times.
Lift both shoulders to your ears, hold 3 seconds, and drop them with a breath out. Repeat 3 times.

name: Grounding Affirmations
description: Interrupt negative spirals with simple, honest statements of strength.

steps.instruction: Take a breath and say slowly: 'I am safe right now.'
Take another breath and say: 'I have handled hard moments before.'
Say: 'This feeling is temporary. It will pass.'
Say: 'I am doing my best and that is enough.'
Take one final slow breath. Let the words settle.

name: Mindful Walk
description: Walking slowly with full attention grounds racing thoughts in your body.

steps.instruction: Stand and feel your feet on the floor. Take a breath.
Begin walking slower than usual. With each step, feel your heel, then the ball of your foot, then your toes.
Notice the small movements that keep you balanced. Your arms, your hips.
Breathe naturally. If thoughts arrive, return your attention to the sensation of each step.
Walk for 3 minutes. Return to stillness. Notice how your mind feels quieter.

name: Warm Hands Meditation
description: Directing blood flow into your hands activates the relaxation response.

steps.instruction: Sit quietly. Rest your hands face-up on your thighs.
Close your eyes. Imagine warmth spreading into your palms and fingers — like sitting near a fire.
With each exhale, imagine that warmth deepening. Let your hands feel heavy and warm.
Hold this image for 2 minutes. Notice if your hands actually warm slightly. Open your eyes slowly.

name: Eye Palming
description: Rest strained eyes and signal safety to your nervous system.

steps.instruction: Rub your palms together briskly for 10 seconds to generate warmth.
Cup your warm palms gently over your closed eyes — no pressure on the eyes themselves.
Breathe slowly. Let your eyes relax into total darkness. Stay for 60–90 seconds.
Slowly remove your hands. Let light return gradually. Blink gently. Take a breath.

name: Humming Exhale
description: Humming on the exhale activates the vagus nerve for fast calm.

steps.instruction: Sit comfortably. Take a normal breath in through your nose.
On the exhale, hum softly — mmmmm — for the full exhale. Feel the vibration in your chest and face.
Inhale again. Hum on the exhale. Make the hum as long as comfortable.
Repeat 6–8 times. With each hum, let your body soften a little more.

name: Feelings Check-In
description: Writing about emotions helps process them and reduces their intensity.

steps.instruction: Find something to write with — phone notes, paper, anything.
Write: 'Right now I feel ___' and fill it in honestly. Don't edit, just write.
Write: 'This is probably because ___'. Again, don't judge, just name what comes up.
Write: 'One small thing that might help me right now is ___'.
Read what you wrote. Take a breath. You've just processed something real.
